title: Sore throat
summary: Most sore throats accompanying a cold are viral and settle within several days. Pain control, fluids and local soothing are more useful than antibiotics unless testing or examination identifies a bacterial cause.
evidenceSnapshot: Paracetamol or ibuprofen can reduce pain. Gargles, lozenges and warm drinks are mainly comfort measures with modest or uncertain trial evidence.

interventions.name: Paracetamol
interventions.finding: Reliably relieves sore-throat pain and fever when used correctly.
interventions.practical: Adults and people aged 12+ should not exceed 1 g every 4–6 hours or 4 g total in 24 hours; lower limits may apply with liver disease, low body weight, heavy alcohol use or frailty.
interventions.mechanism: Reduces central pain and fever signalling.
interventions.cautions: Check every cold-and-flu product for hidden paracetamol.

interventions.name: Ibuprofen
interventions.finding: Can reduce throat pain, fever and inflammation.
interventions.practical: Follow the pack; take with food if it upsets the stomach.
interventions.cautions: Avoid or seek advice with kidney disease, ulcers, anticoagulants, some asthma, dehydration, pregnancy or cardiovascular disease.

interventions.name: Saltwater gargle
interventions.finding: Often feels soothing, but high-quality evidence for changing illness duration is limited.
interventions.practical: Dissolve about 1/2 teaspoon of salt in a glass of warm water, gargle and spit out. Do not swallow.
interventions.mechanism: May temporarily reduce mucosal swelling and loosen secretions.
interventions.cautions: Not suitable for young children who cannot gargle safely.

interventions.name: Medicated lozenges or sprays
interventions.finding: Local anaesthetic, anti-inflammatory or antiseptic products may provide short-term pain relief.
interventions.practical: Use exactly as labelled and choose age-appropriate products.
interventions.mechanism: Temporarily numbs or reduces local irritation.
interventions.cautions: Can mask worsening symptoms; some ingredients cause allergy or numbness that affects swallowing.

interventions.name: Warm or cool fluids
interventions.finding: Temperature preference varies; both can reduce discomfort and support hydration.
interventions.practical: Choose what feels best rather than forcing hot drinks.
interventions.mechanism: Moistens the throat and reduces irritation.

practicalSteps: Drink regularly even if appetite is low.
Use simple pain relief before meals or sleep if needed.
Avoid smoke, alcohol and foods that sharply irritate the throat.
Consider testing/medical review when strep, glandular fever, COVID-19 or another cause is plausible.
avoidOrCare: Difficulty swallowing saliva, drooling, a muffled voice or one-sided swelling needs urgent assessment.
Persistent white patches may have causes other than strep, including thrush.

myths.claim: Any white spots mean antibiotics are needed.
myths.reality: Viral tonsillitis, glandular fever and thrush can also cause white patches. Diagnosis requires the whole clinical picture and sometimes testing.
Safe warm drinks may soothe; temperatures capable of killing a virus would burn tissue.

redFlags: Breathing difficulty, drooling or inability to swallow fluids
Severe one-sided throat pain, neck swelling or muffled 'hot potato' voice
Stiff neck, non-blanching rash, confusion or severe dehydration
Symptoms that persist, rapidly worsen or recur
unsettled: The clinical importance of antiseptic throat sprays is uncertain; symptom relief does not establish faster recovery.
